speaker: interviewer
text: It's August the 10th, 1995. The survivor is Allen Greenstein. Ellen Baskin-- interviewer. It's North Hollywood, California, USA. It will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 10, 1995. We're interviewing Allen Greenstein. My name is Ellen Baskin. We're in North Hollywood, California in the United States of America. And the language will be English. I'd like to begin by asking you to tell me your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Allen Greenstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-L-L-E-N G-R-E-E-N-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born Abram Grynsztin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. A-B-R-A-M, Abram. That's in Polish. And Grynsztin. G-R-Y-N-S-Z-T-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born December 25th, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am close to 70 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born Opatow, Poland, in the province Kielce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just spell Opatow and Kielce?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. O-P-A-T-O-W. And the province, Kielce-- K-I-E-L-C-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the community like when you were growing up there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The community was about 7,000 Jews. Little community, about 30 kilometers west of the Vistula. And it was between Ostrowiec and Kielce, which was the province. It was 61 kilometers to Kielce. It was 17 kilometers to Ostrowiec, which had a railroad station. And Sandomierz was 30 kilometers, which was east to-- close to the Vistula river. And there were other small communities close by. Mainly, around the city, was farmers-- Poles, you know, what they-- worked on their farms. And they came in-- Wednesday, they came into the city to sell their ware, chickens, and all kinds of stuff-- eggs and butter. And they bought stuff, like clothing, boots. And whatever they needed for their home, they bought in the city. This was market day-- every Wednesday.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Yosef-- [? Yehuda. ?] My mother's was Esther. We lived on number 9 [? Waska ?] Street, which means narrow street. Was another street-- the main street in Opatow was Berka Joselewicza, which was named after a Jewish general from the First World War. And we had four synagogues-- four Beit Midrash and one synagogue. The synagogue was 400 years old. It was built at the same time as the church was built. The first Jews that settled in Opatow was in the 1400s or 1500s. It was one of the first three cities in Poland which allowed Jews to settle in that community.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had your family been in that community?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as my family is concerned, me and my father was born there. My grandfather was born there. Further than that, I don't know because I didn't know my grandfather. Because I'm named after him. And my father was born in 1869 in Opatow. My oldest brother was born in 1891 in the same city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was David. And I had-- we were nine brothers and three sisters in the family. And most of my family were-- went with the [INAUDIBLE], with the, you know, to Treblinka. Some of us were in concentration camps, labor camps. One of my brothers, Aaron, he died on-- in 1945, on the way from Auschwitz to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the names of all your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My oldest brother's name was David, next to him was Pinchas. After him, was a sister, Yittela. After that sister, it was another brother, Baruch. Than another brother, Shimon. And then a-- a sister, Yohevet. Then my next brother's name was Moshe. He died in 1939 in the Polish army, defending Warsaw. As a matter of fact, it was in Modlin-- which was place where he died. And next to him-- him was Aaron, then Shmuel Maier, and then Benjamin, myself, and then I had a younger sister-- two years younger-- her name was Golda. My mother's name was Esther. And she and my sister and-- went away in-- in 1942-- '41 in October, with the [INAUDIBLE] from our hometown to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your father had been married once before?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my-- this was his second marriage. And my-- the first wife died. He had six children with his first wife. We were from the second marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was the first child that your mother gave birth to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe. Moshe, Aaron, then Shmuel Maier, Benjamin, myself, and my sister. We lived in a-- we had our own house. We lived in this-- we were all born in the same house. We had our own backyard with our own outhouse, which a lot of people didn't have. In the-- on the street, they had to go to the public-- [? yes, ?] about a mile away. We had Paul living in our-- on one of the rooms in the back. And he took care, like bringing water every day to the house. And every Thursday, they came down and washed the floors, you know, preparing for Shabbat. In the wintertime, they used to come down on Saturday and put down the [? oven ?,] you know, to make fire in the oven. Because us, Jews, were not allowed to make a fire on Sabbath. And that's what-- and they cleaned around the house. They're-- they had some basement outside where they kept their stuff. We had a basement that was filled up with potatoes, you know, for the winter, beets, all kinds of things. We like were self-sufficient. We had-- outside, we had stacks with wood. We didn't have to go and buy. At our place, was we prepared all those things in the summertime. And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We had flour in the house. We had sugar in the house. The only thing my mother had to go out for is to buy a few things, because milk was delivered by a woman every morning. As far as eggs, butter, and all the other things, for-- was bought on Wednesdays when the farmers came into town. And we baked our own bread, once a week. Not in-- in the house. We baked it at the bakery. At home, we prepared the door and everything, then we took it over to the bakery to have it baked there. And when the war broke out, I was 14 years old. I just finished public school. The-- the first winter when the Germans occupied the city, we were forced into clean the-- the roads for snow for be able for the army to move because there was a very heavy winter. And we had-- the-- the Judenrat had to supply young men to go to work. Then after they went there, the first-- in 1940, we started working on the highways, building highways for the military and working in the stone quarries. A group of 20 young boys-- the same age as me, maybe a year or two years older. Every day, we were marched from a-- a point in the city, where we assembled. And one of the [INAUDIBLE] went with us out to the-- 6 kilometers away, to a stone quarry where we worked there, eight hours a day, and came home. We got some-- some pay from the Judenrat for our labor there. And the-- 1940, '41, we worked on the highways-- building highways, putting stones and the asphalt on the highways from Opatow to Sandomierz and the other ways around. That's how I worked in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: If we could go back, just for a minute, to before the war started, I was wondering if you could talk about when you first became aware of Nazis and the uprisings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I first became aware, as a child, because one of my brothers lived in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baruch. And my father went to visit him in 1933. And I remember when he came back, he was telling us that one evening, when they were going home from services, Shavuout night, that three Nazi in their brown shirts, approached them. My father had a long, white beard. And they stopped him. They said, [SPEAKING GERMAN] This was the first time, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: We'll-- we'll do things to the Jews. And that was the first time that we-- I heard the, you know, the problems about the Nazis in Germany. And then in 1938, after Crystal Night, my brother was taken in-- in Germany-- who lived in Breslau, in Germany, was taken to Buchenwald concentration camp. And he spent a few months there till he got some papers that he's allowed-- he will be able to leave Germany because he was a [INAUDIBLE]. He didn't have no citizenship in Germany because he came in 1918. He escaped from the Polish army, and he went to Germany, you know, to serve in the army in 1918. And he lived in Germany all those years. And it so happened that the day before the war broke out, he was able to leave Germany. And he went to England. He joined the English army, and he was sent back to the continent. And I don't remember what month it was that he was in Dunkirk when they evacuated the English army. And he was evacuated back to England from Belgium-- I think Dunkirk is in Belgium. And he was evacuated to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had your dealings been with-- with non-Jews in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I went to school with non-Jews. I went to public school for seven years. I have graduated from public school in 1939. I mean, we always had fights. There was anti-Semitism. But we lived with it. We knew what it is. And mostly, we lived by ourselves. It was like a country in your own country because you obey the law of the state, which was of the Polish government, and we had our own Jewish laws, you know, that we had to follow, and like religious laws, and things like that. Everything, as far as courts was concerned, was that any dispute between Jews, was settled by the rabbi back then. And, we had-- like I said before, we had [INAUDIBLE] immigration. We had a yeshivah. And also we had shul, which was built in, you know-- it was 400 years old. It was a massive building with big, thick walls. On the outside, it didn't look like much, but when you came inside-- the height of it with the center stage where the-- where you read the Torah, and [? what was ?] the steps to go up. 18 steps on each side to go up. And then the front where the [INAUDIBLE] was, where the chazan, you know, where the service is. There were steps where is choir, they was standing there. And the floor was made out of tiles. And it was never heated because you're not allowed-- allowed to heat the synagogue. You're not allowed to heat it, so in wintertime, it was pretty cold. And we had a very famous [? rabbi ?] in the city. What-- the one of them, his great, great, great grandson was here in America, marching in the-- with him-- with Martin Luther King.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Yeshua Heschel. His great, great, great grandfather's name was Rabbi [? Scheuer ?] Heschel [? Memezebesch, ?] which, in Hebrew, the called him [SPEAKING HEBREW]. And he was famous at home. And then the next rabbi, after this one, we had Rabbi [? Meier. ?] He was called, [SPEAKING HEBREW]. And in his-- in his [INAUDIBLE] site, the day when he died, it was remembered. People from all over Poland came to the city. Wrote little notes, you know, and put it down on his-- on his-- where he was-- he had a special house built for him, you know, for-- where he was buried. And that's where people put those-- all those requisitions--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember when he died because he died, you know, in the 1700s. But people, in my time, were still observing, you know, the day that he died. From all over Poland, they used to come. And the city was full of life-- with the scribes sitting in the city to write, you know, notes to go out and put down. The notes-- you know, everybody [? requested ?] [? it. ?] It's the same way as, you know, they do it in Kotel Maraavi, in Jerusalem. The same thing happened the days when-- yes. I mean, the city consisted of-- it was divided the upper part, and the lower part, and it was four streets. Then when had-- in my time, we had a beautiful center of the city. It was called the mark. It had nice stores, and it was a beautiful garden built, you know, and where the animal kept in there. And there were benches around. There were people used to-- to walk and sit-- and have benches to sit down. And it came, a Friday afternoon, all the stores were closed because 99% of-- were Jewish owned. And everybody-- not everybody went to services, but about 90% of the people were observant. I remember we used to go, my father to Friday night services, then, when we came home, we sat down to the table. Nine people, you know, to have a Friday night meal and sing Zemirot. [PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, those are things that you can't-- [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: What you can't forget Saturday mornings, when we went to services. My father had friends that used to come in-- [CRYING] Saturday morning. And we had Shabbat tea, you know, before going to services because you're not allowed to eat. And coming on for the services, you know, the Shabbat meal consisted of cholent. You know, we sat down to eat and sing, you know, Zemirot. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That's how we spent, you know, a Saturday-- relaxing, nobody worked. Sure, there were certain people in the city but they-- didn't observe-- especially the young at the time. And I remember the Socialist movement, the Communist movement in the-- in the '30s. There was a-- it was a cultural city. We had a Hebrew school, a Tarbut. We had a Mizrachi. I went to the Mizrachi school. We had a Talmud Torah, and we had a yeshivah. And we had a bibliotek and we had this artists. We had shows performed by locals or, also, came in troops from Warsaw. You know Morrie Schwartz-- I remember when they played the-- the play, it called [INAUDIBLE] and other plays. It was very cultural, a very nice city. We had nice places to go on the summer-- like behind the cemetary, the Christian cemetery. There was a hilly place and the water. Or it was on the other side of town, going towards-- from the Jewish cemetery back, was also a river, Opatowska, named after the city. And we used to, you know, have places where we could dip in. I mean, the water wasn't that deep that you were afraid you would drown. We had bridges, you know, that connected through those rivers. We had a big factory-- Mandelbaum's factory-- to produce soap. And another factory, which, from all the-- the things that the farmers brought in that made oil. And otherwise, most of the people were small-- you know, with their small, little business, little stores. They were workers, like tailors, which they-- they made their ware in the-- once a week was the market in our town. On Monday, they went to another one, to Sandomierz. Thursday, they went to Lagow and things like that because, otherwise, just sitting at home, they couldn't have made their living not going out and trying to sell their merchandise.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had six people in the house working. My father was producing boots, and most of our business was done with farmers. We had like this-- I mean, a clientele for 20, 30 years with the same people. They come in, then my father knew what they need. And we didn't go any place. My father didn't travel. Everything was in the house because we had a big enough place. The house consisted of, you know, two bedrooms, a big room, and then a kitchen, and then one place-- it was like a front after this, where the-- where the work was done. Two of my brothers worked. And we had about two-- two other guys-- Gentile guys, Christian-- but they worked for us. And this was all the years that I remember, what-- what was done. The-- the city was pleasant place. I mean, there were nice buildings. They were small-- I mean, two or three-- two stories was the tallest building. There wasn't any high buildings. We had a case where-- what I said, Rabbi [? Scheuer ?] Heschel had was a synagogue which was upstairs. And it was a story when during the 1700s, when the Hasidim were coming down, Friday night, from the services, their hats were thrown off by the demons or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- he asked to-- they should take a piece of steel. And they engraved his name, and they put down on the top of the-- the attic in the synagogue. And after they-- it was the-- under-- the name was Rabbi [? Scheuer ?] Heschel [? Memezebesch ?].

speaker: interviewee
text: And after they-- the thing stopped-- I remember when I used to go there with my father on Saturday afternoons in the summer to learn [INAUDIBLE]. As young kids, we used to climb up on the step ladder and to look in to see-- that-- it was called a [INAUDIBLE], but actually, it was a piece of steel. It was about four inches wide, which his name was engraved in it. And if they were-- people came to visit the city. They old-- the oldest used to bring down to show it-- what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was still there when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was still there when I was there. I mean, we used to go up and see it. And all of that came at the end, you know, in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of the beginning of the war, in September of '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that Friday morning-- I think it was September the 3rd-- we heard on the radio that the Germans invaded Poland. And we heard on the radio, nadchodzi, nadchodzi-- that the planes are coming, you know, closer by. And three days later, they bombed-- a few bombs fell on our hometown because you could see people streaming east. You know, from the west of the country, streaming east, towards the side of the Vistula. And you saw the units of the army, you know, in disarray, going back. And then you saw-- in Tuesday-- Thursday, the next-- Friday started the war and Thursday afternoon, the first Germans arrived in the city. The same night, half of the city, the main part of the city, was burned down. They claimed that somebody, that one of the Poles fired a shot and that gave them the excuse to burn down part of the city. And they burned the City Hall, which was about a mile away from that part. And the next couple of days, they assembled most of the people, men and-- only men, into a-- a hole, which was the fireman's hole, like where the theatres used to be played. They assembled the people, and they kept them there till their random was paid for-- to let them out. This was the first week when the Germans occupied the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father taken then?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was taken in there. And they spent there, I think about two days, they spent in there. And then they released them after it was arranged with them. The city was also a powiat, which means like a district government. It wasn't just a city government, it was also like-- here, I think you would call it a county. The same thing that was a county. There was an impressive building in there where the-- then, when the Germans came in, they took over all those offices. You know, and they were direct in their business. Then there was another part of the city-- that was where the [INAUDIBLE] were stationed. And they-- they were the security. And then we had the SA-- SD. Also those were security police. And the first few months, I mean, there was a lot of refugees streamed in from the east, from Kalisz, from Poznan, and from all those things. And people from the biggest cities started coming in because where we were, food was no problem because the farmers were all around the city. So you could-- the farmers were allowed to come in, even after they created the ghetto in 1941. We couldn't go out, but the Poles could come in with their ware and sell. If you had the money to buy, you were able. So they came in. And so-- the city increased by about 4,000 or 5,000 people because of the influx of those that came in from the other, other bigger cities.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they-- they stayed in crowded places because they had to crowd in wherever they could. And then they formed a kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to break to--
